screens.name: landing
screens.expectations: A polished marketing landing screen for a paid lawn-irrigation planning app, as rendered in a real 1920x1080 desktop browser window (a plain screenshot of the page itself, not a marketing mockup wrapped in a laptop/monitor bezel graphic — grade the page layout only). Must show: a clear app name/brand, a hero value proposition (water/money savings), a single obvious primary call-to-action button to start (e.g. 'Load My Property'), and no visible debug/test/placeholder UI. Should look premium and trustworthy enough that a homeowner would pay for the Pro export, not like an unfinished prototype, and should not look like a phone-sized layout stranded in the middle of a huge blank screen.

screens.name: planner-empty
screens.expectations: The lawn planning screen as rendered in a real 1920x1080 desktop browser window, right after entering the planner, before anything is drawn. Must show a map/grid area to draw on that makes good use of the available screen width and height (not a small fixed-size box with large empty margins), a toolbar for switching between zone and head tools, and clear instruction text telling the user to draw their lawn. Should feel like a real design tool built for a large screen, not a mobile layout stretched or centered awkwardly.

screens.name: zone-drawn
screens.expectations: A drawn lawn zone shown as a shaded/colored polygon on the map, sized to make reasonable use of a large desktop map canvas (not a tiny square stranded in a huge empty grid), with the toolbar now offering head placement. Should look clean and precise, not glitchy or overlapping.

screens.name: auto-placed-plan
screens.expectations: The full results screen as rendered in a real 1920x1080 desktop browser window after auto-placing sprinkler heads: sprinkler head markers inside the drawn zone, a prominent 'Save Up To' annual dollar savings figure, a property breakdown, and a monetization/upsell card (Pro Plan / shop list). This is the core paid-value screen — it must look premium, trustworthy, and worth paying for, with clean typography and spacing, good use of the large desktop viewport, and no large awkward areas of unused whitespace beyond what's normal for a moderately-sized single-zone test plan on a big map canvas.

screens.name: edit-head-panel
screens.expectations: A single sprinkler head has been placed and selected, opening an 'Edit Head' panel with controls (radius slider, arc/type selectors, a remove action). The panel should look like a clean, purpose-built editing tool with clear labels, not a raw unstyled form.

screens.name: checkout-cancel-toast
screens.expectations: A toast/banner is shown telling the user checkout was cancelled. It should look like a deliberate, calm, non-alarming notice — not a jarring red error or browser-alert style message, since cancelling isn't the user's fault and shouldn't feel like a failure.
